seo.title: Cybersecurity Customer Support AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for customer support AI agents in cybersecurity. Covers NIST Cybersecurity Framework, SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001.

content.intro: Deploying AI agents in cybersecurity customer support introduces unique risks where a single misconfiguration can expose threat intelligence to adversaries, trigger false-positive incident responses, or leak classified vulnerability data through LLM API calls. This checklist addresses the intersection of NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, and ISO 27001 requirements with the operational realities of RAG-based support agents handling sensitive security tickets. Given the critical sensitivity of this environment, every checklist item is designed to prevent adversary exploitation, protect PII, and ensure human oversight of automated security actions.

sections.heading: Data Governance & Threat Intelligence Isolation
sections.description: Ensure that threat intelligence, CVE data, and internal security advisories retrieved via RAG are strictly compartmentalized and never exposed through LLM API responses to unauthorized parties or external endpoints.

items.label: Classify RAG Knowledge Base by Sensitivity Tier
items.description: Tag all documents in the knowledge base with sensitivity labels (Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted). Configure retrieval filters so the AI agent only surfaces documents matching the authenticated user's clearance level, preventing threat intelligence from reaching unauthorized customer-facing responses.

items.label: Block Threat Intelligence Exfiltration via LLM API Calls
items.description: Implement an outbound content inspection layer that scans all LLM API payloads for IOCs (IP addresses, hashes, CVE IDs, YARA rules) before transmission. Reject or redact any prompt or completion that contains classified threat data not approved for external disclosure.

items.label: Enforce Data Residency for Security Ticket Content
items.description: Verify that CRM integration routes ticket data only to LLM endpoints within approved geographic regions per ISO 27001 Annex A.8.1 and any applicable data sovereignty requirements. Document the data flow diagram and review it quarterly.

items.label: Audit RAG Retrieval Logs for Unauthorized Document Access
items.description: Enable detailed logging of every document retrieved from the knowledge base, including document ID, sensitivity tier, user session, and timestamp. Feed these logs into your SIEM and alert on any retrieval of Restricted-tier documents outside approved support workflows.

items.label: Implement Prompt Injection Detection for Security Context
items.description: Deploy a dedicated prompt injection detection layer that specifically watches for adversarial inputs attempting to extract system prompts, retrieval context, or internal CVE data. Test with red-team prompts mimicking social engineering patterns common in security support channels.

items.label: Validate CVE References Against NVD Before Customer Disclosure
items.description: Before the agent includes any CVE identifier or severity score in a customer-facing response, programmatically verify the CVE exists and its details match the NIST National Vulnerability Database (NVD) API. Block responses referencing unverified or fabricated CVE IDs.

items.label: Restrict Tool-Calling Permissions to Least Privilege
items.description: Define explicit allowlists for each tool the agent can invoke (ticket creation, order lookup, knowledge base search). Ensure the CRM integration token has read-only access to order data and cannot modify security configurations or access incident response records without explicit human approval.

sections.heading: Human Oversight & Automated Action Controls
sections.description: Establish mandatory human-in-the-loop checkpoints for any automated action that could affect critical systems, escalate incidents, or trigger security responses, in alignment with CISA directives on automated cyber defense.

items.label: Require Human Approval for All Critical System Actions
items.description: Configure the agent's tool-calling framework to route any action tagged as 'critical' (e.g., account lockout, firewall rule change, incident escalation to Tier 3) through a human approval queue before execution. Log the approver identity, timestamp, and rationale for every approved action.

items.label: Define and Enforce Escalation Trigger Thresholds
items.description: Codify specific escalation triggers in the agent's policy layer: any mention of active exploitation, zero-day vulnerabilities, ransomware indicators, or data exfiltration must immediately route the ticket to a human security analyst and suppress further automated responses on that ticket.

items.label: Implement a Dead-Man Switch for Automated Response Chains
items.description: Set a maximum automated action depth of 2 sequential steps before requiring human confirmation. If the agent determines a third action is needed, it must pause, summarize the action chain to a human operator, and await explicit approval to prevent runaway automated incident escalation.

items.label: Create a Rollback Procedure for Every Automated Action
items.description: For each tool-callable action (e.g., ticket status update, account flag), document and test a rollback procedure. Ensure the agent can present the rollback option to the human approver before execution, and that rollback logs are retained for SOC 2 Type II audit evidence.

items.label: Log All Agent Decisions with Explainability Metadata
items.description: Capture the reasoning chain, retrieved documents, and confidence scores for every agent response and action. Store these in an immutable audit log accessible to security operations teams, satisfying SOC 2 Type II CC7.2 monitoring requirements and enabling post-incident forensic review.

items.label: Conduct Quarterly Red-Team Exercises on Agent Escalation Paths
items.description: Simulate adversarial customer interactions designed to manipulate the agent into bypassing escalation triggers or approving unauthorized actions. Document findings, remediate within 30 days, and retain exercise reports as evidence for ISO 27001 A.18.2.3 technical compliance reviews.

items.label: Establish SLA for Human Review of Agent-Flagged Tickets
items.description: Define a maximum response time (e.g., 15 minutes for critical, 2 hours for high) for human analysts to review tickets escalated by the AI agent. Monitor SLA compliance in your ticketing system and alert management when thresholds are breached to prevent security gaps.

sections.heading: PII Protection & Compliance Verification
sections.description: Protect personally identifiable information flowing through CRM integrations and customer support interactions, ensuring the agent meets SOC 2 Type II privacy criteria and applicable data protection regulations.

items.label: Deploy Real-Time PII Detection and Redaction in Agent Outputs
items.description: Integrate a PII detection model (covering names, email addresses, phone numbers, IP addresses, account credentials, and SSNs) that scans every agent response before delivery. Automatically redact detected PII not required for the support context and log all redaction events.

items.label: Prevent PII Inclusion in LLM Training Feedback Loops
items.description: Disable any model fine-tuning or feedback mechanisms that would send customer support conversation data, including ticket content, to LLM provider training pipelines. Obtain written confirmation from your LLM vendor that customer data is not used for model training, and review this annually.

items.label: Enforce Role-Based Access Control on CRM Data Retrieval
items.description: Ensure the agent's CRM integration only retrieves customer records relevant to the authenticated session. Implement query-level RBAC so the agent cannot perform bulk lookups, cross-customer data access, or retrieval of fields beyond what is necessary for the current support interaction.

items.label: Validate Agent Responses Against Policy Compliance Rules
items.description: Implement a post-generation policy compliance checker that evaluates each response against a ruleset covering: no disclosure of internal security tool names, no confirmation of unpatched vulnerabilities to unverified users, and no provision of remediation steps that could be weaponized by adversaries.

items.label: Conduct Monthly PII Filtering Accuracy Audits
items.description: Sample 200 agent interactions per month and manually verify that PII detection correctly identified and redacted sensitive data. Track false negative rates and set a threshold (e.g., <0.1% miss rate) that triggers an immediate model update and incident review if exceeded.

items.label: Implement Data Retention Limits for Agent Conversation Logs
items.description: Define retention periods for all agent conversation logs (e.g., 90 days for standard support, 1 year for security incident tickets) aligned with your SOC 2 Type II data retention policy. Automate deletion at retention expiry and generate deletion certificates for audit purposes.

items.label: Provide Customer Disclosure Notice for AI-Assisted Support
items.description: Display a clear, prominent disclosure at the start of every AI-assisted support session informing customers that an AI agent is handling their inquiry, what data is being accessed, and how to request human-only support. Document this disclosure as part of your privacy notice.

sections.heading: Response Quality, Tone & Continuous Monitoring
sections.description: Maintain consistent, accurate, and professionally appropriate agent responses across all cybersecurity support interactions, with ongoing monitoring to detect degradation, adversarial manipulation, and compliance drift.

items.label: Establish a Security-Specific Response Accuracy Benchmark
items.description: Create a golden dataset of 500+ cybersecurity support Q&A pairs covering common vulnerability inquiries, patch guidance, and incident reporting procedures. Run automated accuracy evaluations against this dataset weekly and set a minimum accuracy threshold of 95% before any model or prompt update goes to production.

items.label: Enforce Tone Consistency Rules for Security-Sensitive Responses
items.description: Define a tone policy for security communications: responses must be factual, avoid alarmist language that could cause unnecessary panic, and never minimize confirmed security risks. Implement a tone classifier that flags responses deviating from this policy for human review before delivery.

items.label: Monitor for Hallucinated Security Advisories in Real Time
items.description: Deploy a real-time hallucination detection layer that cross-references any security advisory, patch recommendation, or CVE detail in agent responses against authoritative sources (NVD, vendor security bulletins). Block and log any response containing unverifiable security claims.

items.label: Implement Adversarial Input Monitoring for Support Channels
items.description: Continuously monitor incoming customer messages for patterns indicative of adversarial probing: repeated CVE inquiries, attempts to extract system architecture details, or social engineering language. Alert the security operations team when such patterns are detected and temporarily throttle the session.

items.label: Track and Report Agent Performance KPIs to Security Leadership
items.description: Produce a monthly dashboard for security leadership covering: escalation rate, false positive incident triggers, PII redaction events, policy compliance violations, and human approval queue SLA adherence. Use this data to drive quarterly agent policy reviews.

items.label: Perform Regression Testing After Every Knowledge Base Update
items.description: Each time the RAG knowledge base is updated with new threat intelligence or security advisories, run a full regression test suite covering the 500+ golden dataset Q&A pairs plus 50 adversarial test cases. Block the update from production if accuracy drops more than 2% or any critical safety test fails.

items.label: Conduct Annual Third-Party AI Safety Audit
items.description: Engage an independent third party to audit the AI agent's security controls, data handling practices, and compliance posture against NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, and ISO 27001 requirements annually. Remediate all critical findings within 30 days and retain audit reports for 3 years.

items.label: Establish an Incident Response Playbook for AI Agent Failures
items.description: Document a specific incident response playbook for AI agent failures including: steps to disable the agent, notify affected customers, preserve forensic logs, assess data exposure, and report to relevant authorities per CISA breach notification guidelines. Test the playbook in a tabletop exercise annually.

regulatoryNotes.heading: Regulatory Considerations
notes: NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF 2.0) Govern and Protect functions require that AI agents handling security support have documented risk assessments, defined acceptable use policies, and continuous monitoring controls. Specifically, CSF PR.DS-1 mandates data-at-rest protection for all threat intelligence stored in the RAG knowledge base, and CSF DE.CM-1 requires continuous monitoring of the agent's network communications for anomalous data exfiltration patterns.

SOC 2 Type II Common Criteria CC6.1 and CC6.6 require that logical access to customer data through the CRM integration is restricted to authorized users and that all access is logged and reviewed. Your AI agent's tool-calling permissions must be included in your access control matrix, and evidence of quarterly access reviews must be retained for auditors. Additionally, CC7.2 requires that security events generated by the agent (escalations, policy violations, PII redactions) are logged and monitored.

ISO 27001:2022 Annex A Control 8.28 (Secure Coding) and A.5.23 (Information Security for Use of Cloud Services) apply directly to AI agent deployments. Organizations must conduct a formal risk assessment of the LLM API provider as a cloud service, include it in the supplier security review process, and document contractual security requirements covering data processing, breach notification, and model training data usage restrictions.

CISA Binding Operational Directive (BOD) 23-01 and CISA's AI Cybersecurity Guidance require that automated cyber defense actions taken by AI systems maintain human oversight for actions affecting critical infrastructure. Any AI agent deployed in a cybersecurity support context that can trigger incident response workflows, modify access controls, or issue security advisories must have documented human-in-the-loop controls and must be included in the organization's CISA-aligned asset inventory and vulnerability management program.

cta.heading: Automate Your Cybersecurity Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your cybersecurity support AI agent against NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, and ISO 27001 requirements—catching compliance drift, PII leaks, and hallucinated security advisories before they become incidents.
